title: Unit 18: Passive Voice and Deponent Verbs - Grammar Podcast

script.text: Unit 18 introduces 'Type C' verbs, identifying them by their morphological ending '-omai'. These constructions encompass both Deponent verbs and the Passive Voice. Athina, what marks a deponent verb as distinct?
Τα αποθετικά ρήματα μοιάζουν με παθητικά, αλλά έχουν ενεργητική σημασία. Έρχομαι, χρειάζομαι, σκέφτομαι.

Correct. They occupy the morphosyntactic space of passive verbs while performing purely active roles. Additionally, we must conjugate these verbs accurately. Athina, please provide the second and third person singular.
Έρχεσαι και έρχεται. Και μην ξεχνάτε το 'ερχόμαστε' με την αλλαγή του τόνου.

Precisely. The accentual shift in the first person plural is essential. Furthermore, the true Passive Voice—and its reflexive applications—is highly productive in Modern Greek. One can use the preposition 'Apo' plus the Accusative case to denote the agent of the action.
Το βιβλίο διαβάζεται από τον Γιώργο. Ο Γιώργος είναι η αιτία της δράσης.
Regarding the Past Passive Aorist: we witness a new morphological paradigm featuring the suffix '-hka'. Athina, how do the stem groups Σ, Ξ, and Ψ influence these past passive forms?
Ετοιμάστηκα από το σίγμα. Κρύφτηκα από το ψι. Ανοίχτηκα από το ξι.
Correct. This concludes our academic overview of Unit 18. Diligent practice of these passive paradigms is essential for syntactic accuracy in more complex Modern Greek sentences.
